speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling a bit off lately—tired, some swelling in my ankles, and I’ve had a low-grade fever on and off.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Based on your history and today’s evaluation, your primary diagnosis is Essential Hypertension, first documented on July 11th.

speaker: patient
text: Hypertension? I didn’t realize it was that serious—I’ve just been monitoring my numbers at home.

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s review your vitals and labs from today. Your blood glucose came back at 11.7 mmol/L—elevated, especially if you’re fasting.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure reading today is 101 mmHg.

speaker: doctor
text: And systolic is 114 mmHg—so overall BP is 114 over 101.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature is 37.8 °C—mildly elevated.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, that matches what I’ve been feeling—the swelling in my lower limbs has gotten worse over the past few days.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll need to investigate that further. For now, I’m continuing Atorvastatin 20mg taken once daily at night to support cardiovascular risk reduction.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been taking that regularly. But my legs still feel heavy, and sometimes I get short of breath walking up stairs.

speaker: doctor
text: Those symptoms—edema, dyspnea—could suggest volume overload or early heart failure, especially with your hypertension and elevated glucose. Let’s repeat the BP in a few minutes to confirm consistency.

speaker: patient
text: Okay. I did check my BP this morning before coming in—it was higher than usual.

speaker: doctor
text: Indeed—your repeat diastolic reading is now 110 mmHg, which is notably higher than the earlier 101 mmHg we recorded.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature has also normalized to 36.7 °C—likely reflecting transient inflammation or measurement variation earlier.

speaker: patient
text: That’s interesting—I actually rechecked my temperature just now with my digital thermometer, and it’s back to 37.8 °C.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s helpful context. Given your persistent hypertension, edema, and now recurrent low-grade fever, we need better BP control and closer renal and cardiac assessment.

speaker: doctor
text: I am going to switch you from your current antihypertensive regimen to Lisinopril 10mg once daily in the morning—this will help reduce afterload and may improve your edema.

speaker: patient
text: Lisinopril? I’ve heard it can cause cough—should I watch for that?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, that’s common. Also monitor for dizziness, especially when standing, and avoid NSAIDs. We’ll check your potassium and creatinine in one week.

speaker: patient
text: Got it. And just to clarify—that 37.8 °C reading I mentioned earlier? That’s the same number as the one you noted at the start—the one from this morning.
